methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: 1X Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $10B

private.name: Bedrock Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.75B

private.name: Dexterity
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.65B

private.name: Collaborative Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Fairmat
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $350M

private.name: GrayMatter Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series B), post-money $300M+

data.narrative:
Tritone Technologies is a Series B robotics and manufacturing company founded by Ofer Ben-Zur. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of 9 private peers and 1 public company operating in the same sector and stage.

Peer-median analysis serves as the primary valuation method. The comp set of 6 same-stage, same-sector robotics firms—including 1X Technologies ($10.0B), Bedrock Robotics ($1.8B), Dexterity ($1.6B), Collaborative Robotics ($500M), and Fairmat ($350M)—yields a median valuation of $1.1B, with a range spanning $300M to $10.0B. This wide dispersion reflects material heterogeneity within the Series B robotics segment, likely driven by differences in technical approach, capital efficiency, and market traction.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $914M to $1.2B, centered on $1.1B. Given that only one valuation method is fully applicable from available data, this range represents the peer-median estimate with reasonable bandwidth but carries elevated uncertainty. Key unknowns affecting precision include Tritone's relative unit economics, deployment scale, and customer concentration relative to the peer set. The wide peer range ($300M–$10.0B) indicates that stage and sector alone are insufficient discriminators; granular operational metrics would materially refine the estimate.
